title: Entangling Power: A Probe of Symmetry and Integrability in Quantum Many-Body Systems
abstract: The entangling power of a unitary operator quantifies its ability to generate entanglement from product states and provides a natural probe of quantum many-body dynamics. Entanglement extremization at points of enhanced symmetry has previously been observed in high-energy scattering. In this work we compute the time-averaged entangling power of anisotropic Heisenberg spin chains across two-site models and finite-size systems, as well as the entangling power of the two-magnon $S$-matrix in the thermodynamic limit. For two-site models we establish a monotonic hierarchy: the entangling power decreases as the symmetry group grows, reaching its minimum at the $SU(2)$ XXX point. Finite-size XXZ chains exhibit sharp dips at the $SU(2)$ points $Δ=\pm 1$ and the free-fermion point $Δ=0$, with the free-fermion dip decaying much more slowly with system size. In the thermodynamic limit, we decompose the two-magnon $S$-matrix into quantum logic gates -- Identity, SWAP, and $σ_z\otimesσ_z$ -- and show that the entangling power vanishes for all scattering energies at the $SU(2)$ points, where the $S$-matrix reduces to the Identity gate, while the free-fermion point achieves the maximum -- the opposite of the finite-size many-body behavior. The entangling power can serve as an {\em operator} diagnostic for symmetry and selected aspects of integrability in quantum simulations of spin-chain dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the ability of a quantum operator to generate entanglement can act as a probe for symmetry and integrability. By studying Heisenberg spin chains, the authors show that higher symmetry leads to a suppression of entangling power. The results bridge the gap between small-scale quantum gates and the thermodynamic limit of many-body systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether the entangling power of a unitary operator can serve as a dynamical diagnostic for symmetry and integrability in quantum many-body systems.
main_result: The study establishes that increasing symmetry suppresses entangling power, showing sharp dips at SU(2) and free-fermion points in finite chains, while the two-magnon S-matrix shows vanishing entangling power at SU(2) points.
method: The authors use an efficient algorithm for computing time-averaged entangling power via the diagonal ensemble, alongside analytical derivations using the operator-state correspondence and the Bethe Ansatz.
model_or_system: Anisotropic Heisenberg (XXZ, XXX, XYZ) spin chains of various sizes (two-site, finite-size, and thermodynamic limit) and the two-magnon S-matrix.
key_observables: Time-averaged entangling power (e_p), linear entropy, and the S-matrix scattering amplitudes.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter (Delta), spin magnitude (s=1/2, 1), system size (L), and rapidity (theta).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes Haar-random product states for the definition of entangling power and focuses on integrable sectors of the XXZ model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows instantaneous entangling power oscillations; Figure 3 illustrates the non-monotonic dependence of e_p on the isospectral parameter q; Figure 4 shows the scaling of the SU(2) dip with system size.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the entangling power concept, analyzes two-site models to establish a symmetry hierarchy, extends the study to finite-size XXZ chains, and concludes with the thermodynamic limit via the two-magnon S-matrix.
why_interesting: It provides a new operator-based diagnostic for detecting symmetry and integrability, which is highly relevant for characterizing the dynamics of programmable quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Instantaneous ep ep(U(t)) of the two-site Hamiltonian (6) as a function of time at/π for the XXX (solid), Ising (dashed), XX (dash-dotted), and XYZ (dotted) models. (a) Spin-1/2 (two-qubit, d = 2). (b) Spin-1 (two-qutrit, d = 3). For the XYZ model we use (ax, ay, az) = (1, 0.7, 0.3). Thin dashed horizontal lines indicate the time-averaged values ep.

FIG. 2: Time-averaged entangling power of the two-site spin- 1 XXZ model as a function of anisotropy ∆. The solid curve is the generic analytic formula, Eq. (18). At ∆= ±1 (SU(2) symmetry) and ∆= 0 (U(1) × Z2 symmetry), eigenvalue de- generacies result in the true ep (filled circles) dropping below the generic curve (open circles). Dashed lines indicate the magnitude of each dip.

FIG. 3: Time-averaged entangling power of the spin-1/2 isospectral family, Eq. (29), as a function of q = C2(|z⟩)/2, where C is the concurrence of the projector state |z⟩. The product-state limit (q = 0, ep = 1/9) and the XXX/Bell-state limit (q = 1/2, ep = 1/12) are marked, together with the max- imum at q = 1/6. The XXX model sits at the minimum of ep over the isospectral family.

FIG. 4: Time-averaged entangling power ep for the spin-1/2 XXZ chain as a function of anisotropy ∆. (a) L = 10 chain, shown on a scale to resolve the residual dip structure near saturation. (b) Overlay of L = 4 (solid blue), 6 (dash-dotted green), 8 (dashed purple), and 10 (solid red). The L = 2 case was already shown in Fig. 2. Root-of-unity fine structure at ∆= ±1/2 becomes visible for L ≥6.
FIG. 5: Dense scan of ep for the spin-1/2 XXZ chain at L = 10 over ∆∈[0.25, 0.85], resolving all four root-of-unity dips. Vertical dashed lines mark the exact algebraic locations.
